questions.question: Which heart block rhythm has the distinguishing feature of a PR interval that measures greater than 0.20 second and measures the same duration each time?
questions.explanation: First degree heart block is the simplest form — every impulse still gets through to the ventricles, just delayed. That delay shows up as a consistently prolonged (but unchanging) PR interval on every beat.

questions.question: Which heart block dysrhythmia is identified by a repetitious prolonging PR interval pattern after each blocked QRS complex?
questions.explanation: Second degree type I (Mobitz I / Wenckebach) has that classic progressive pattern — the PR interval lengthens with each beat until one QRS is dropped entirely, then the cycle restarts.

questions.question: Which heart block dysrhythmia is identified by missing QRS complexes and a consistent PR interval measurement?
questions.explanation: Second degree type II (Mobitz II) drops QRS complexes suddenly and unpredictably, without the warning sign of a progressively lengthening PR interval — the PR interval stays fixed right up until a beat is simply dropped.

questions.question: Which heart block dysrhythmia is identified by regular P-P and R-R intervals that are firing at two distinctly different rates?
questions.explanation: Third degree (complete) heart block means the atria and ventricles are completely disconnected electrically — each keeps its own regular rhythm, but at two entirely independent rates, since no atrial impulses are getting through to the ventricles at all.

questions.question: P-P intervals are _____ with all heart block dysrhythmias.
questions.explanation: Across all heart block types, the SA node keeps firing normally and regularly — the block happens further down at the AV node or below, so the P-P intervals themselves stay regular even when the QRS complexes don't follow along consistently.

questions.question: QRS complexes that measure 0.12 second or greater with a rate between 20 and 40 beats per minute indicate that the impulses causing ventricular depolarization are coming from the:
questions.explanation: A slow rate (20-40 bpm) paired with a wide QRS points to a ventricular escape rhythm — the Purkinje fibers taking over as a last-resort pacemaker because no signal from above is getting through, which is exactly what happens in third degree heart block.

questions.question: What is the typical heart rate range for first degree heart block?
questions.explanation: First degree heart block only delays conduction — it doesn't drop beats or change the underlying pacemaker — so the overall rate typically stays within the normal 60-100 bpm range.

questions.question: Frequent nonconducted QRS complexes are likely to cause signs of:
questions.explanation: Every dropped QRS complex is a heartbeat that didn't happen — enough of those in a row and the heart simply isn't pumping enough blood, leading to the same low cardiac output signs covered in earlier chapters, like dropping blood pressure.

questions.question: Which heart block dysrhythmia has regular P-P and R-R intervals with both having the same rate?
questions.explanation: In first degree heart block, every P wave still conducts through to a QRS complex, just delayed — so the P-P and R-R intervals stay perfectly matched at the same rate, unlike the dropped beats seen in second and third degree blocks.

questions.question: Which heart block dysrhythmia is known as the "classical" heart block?
questions.explanation: Second degree type II (Mobitz II) is often referred to this way because of its clear-cut, predictable pattern of sudden, unwarned dropped beats — a distinctive and clinically significant presentation that typically requires closer monitoring or pacemaker consideration.
